coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots using different precursors and their combination with TiO2 for enhanced photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Tao
Wang, Qi
Guo, Zhaoying
Kuang, Jianlei
Cao, Wenbin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully synthesized by a facile and green hydrothermal method, and further coupled with P25 (commercial TiO2) to form CQDs/P25 heterojunctions for enhanced photocatalytic activity. Two typical precursors, glucose and citric acid were selected as precursors for the synthesis of CQDs. The effects of precursors on the properties of CQDs and the photocatalytic activity of resultant CQDs/P25 was indeed investigated. The TEM, Raman and XPS analysis revealed the good combination of CQDs on the surface of P25. The photodegradation experiments indicated that CQDs/P25 exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance than that of pure P25. In addition, with the better crystallinity of CQDs, the higher photocatalytic activity of CQDs/P25 was achieved. More importantly, based on the comprehensive and detailed discussion, the hydrothermal carbonization process of various precursors and the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of different CQDs/P25 composites were significantly put forward.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
P25
Precursors
Hydrothermal carbonization
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 composites
Preparation and characterization of CQDs/P25 composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CQDs synthesis using citric acid and glucose.
TEM images of a) CQDs-CA, b) CQDs-G. The insets show the HRTEM image of each samples.
Raman spectra recorded on the CQDs-G, CQDs-CA.
Possible hydrothermal formation process of a) CQDs-CA, b) CQDs-G.
Representative TEM images recorded on a) CQDs-CA/P25, b) CQDs-G/P25.
XRD patterns of P25, CQDs-CA/P25 and CQDs-G/P25.
Raman spectra of the P25 and CQDs-G/P25.
XPS survey spectra of CQDs-G/P25: (a) the full scale XPS spectrum, high-resolution XPS spectra of b) C1s, c) Ti2p and d) O1s.
Photoluminescence spectra of P25, CQDs-CA/P25 and CQDs-G/P25 irradiated at 310 nm.
The photocatalytic degradation of phenol under UV light irradiation by P25, CQDs-G/P25 and CQDs-CA/P25.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of phenol over CQDs/P25 under UV light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots using different precursors and their combination with TiO2 for enhanced photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin College, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Tianjin 301830, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin College, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully synthesized by a facile and green hydrothermal method, and further coupled with P25 (commercial TiO2) to form CQDs/P25 heterojunctions for enhanced photocatalytic activity. Two typical precursors, glucose and citric acid were selected as precursors for the synthesis of CQDs. The effects of precursors on the properties of CQDs and the photocatalytic activity of resultant CQDs/P25 was indeed investigated. The TEM, Raman and XPS analysis revealed the good combination of CQDs on the surface of P25. The photodegradation experiments indicated that CQDs/P25 exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance than that of pure P25. In addition, with the better crystallinity of CQDs, the higher photocatalytic activity of CQDs/P25 was achieved. More importantly, based on the comprehensive and detailed discussion, the hydrothermal carbonization process of various precursors and the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of different CQDs/P25 composites were significantly put forward.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the expanding environmental pollution has attracted wide concerns due to its potential threat to human health. It is of great urgency to develop various effective strategies to resolve this problem. Photocatalysis has been regarded as a very promising utilization in the field of waste water treatment, air purification and other environmental governance. Among all the photocatalytic materials, nano titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most attractive one benefiting from its long-term thermodynamic stability, non-toxicity, and relatively efficient photocatalytic activity [1]. However, due to its large band gap (3.2 eV), TiO2 can only be activated in the ultraviolet (UV) region [2]. That is to say, it can only use less than 5% of the total energy of the solar spectrum. Moreover, the rapid recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs leads to less charges involving in photocatalytic reaction and further inhibits the photocatalytic activity and application of TiO2. From this point of view, any routes increasing the life-time of electron-hole pairs can substantially improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. It has been established that coupling TiO2 with noble metal nanomaterials [3,4] or other semiconductor nanomaterials [5–9], such as WO3, ZnO, CdSe, C3N4 and MoS2 to form heterojunctions are efficient to prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs. In Lee et al. [10]’s work, triangular Ag was coupled with P25 to enhanced the photocatalytic activity, and the enhancement could be mainly attributed to the electronic states favorable of charge separation at the interface between metals and semiconductors. In the study conducted by Hu et al. [11], the CdS/P25 photocatalysts showed higher photocatalytic activities for the photocatalytic selective oxidation of alcohols to aldehydes. Kong et al. [6] reported that the modification of P25 by minor ZnO can efficiently improve the photo-induced charge separation and photocatalytic properties for toluene degradation by the heterojunctions formation and multichannel electron/hole transfer and separation. However, considering the relatively high price of noble metal materials and photocorrosion effects of some semiconductor nanomaterials, such as CdS, it is necessary to search new-type nanomaterials to modify TiO2.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with the advantages of low toxicity, low production cost and high chemical stability, have attracted increasing attentions in the photocatalytic field, especially coupled with TiO2 [12–15]. CQDs refer to zero dimensional carbon material with size below 10 nm, which are composed of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and various surface functional groups [16,17]. More significantly, when coupled with TiO2, the excellent conductivity of CQDs promote the transfer of photogenerated electrons, which is beneficial for the separation of electron-hole pairs and the enhancement of TiO2 photocatalytic activity.

To date, various methods [18] have been reported to prepare CQDs, such as chemical ablation, electrochemical carbonization, laser ablation, microwave irradiation, hydrothermal treatment. Comparatively, CQDs synthesized by the hydrothermal carbonization method exhibit more attractive properties including low cost, high efficiency, nontoxicity, environmentally friendly features and so on. In a typical hydrothermal process, a solution of organic precursor is necessary, which is generally transferred to an autoclave and further reacted at high temperature. Up to now, the organic precursors for the synthesis of CQDs have been extensively studied, and various precursors, such as glucose [19], sucrose [20], citric acid [21], chitosan [22], and even orange juice [23] have been reported. However, most researchers only aimed at searching for new-type organic precursors to prepare CQDs, seldom paid close attention on direct differences between various precursors. Actually, the different structures of precursors, such as carbon atom numbers, carbon chain length, functional groups, play crucial role on the hydrothermal carbonization process, resulting in the CQDs with different structures and chemical properties. Therefore, it is of great significance to investigate the effects of precursors on the properties of CQDs, especially on the photocatalytic properties after coupling with TiO2.

Accordingly, in the present work, two typical precursors, citric acid and glucose were selected as carbon sources to synthesize the CQDs by a simple hydrothermal method. The effects of precursors on the morphology and crystallinity of CQDs were indeed investigated. The as-prepared CQDs were further coupled with P25 to prepare the CQDs/TiO2 composites through hydrothermal method. The impact of the properties of CQDs on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 were investigated by photodegradation of phenol under UV light irradiation. Based on the extensive experimental investigations, the hydrothermal carbonization process of various precursors and the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of different CQDs/TiO2 composites were significantly put forward.

All chemicals were analytical-grade and used without further purification. P25 (purity, 99.5%; crystallite size, ~21 nm; polymorphic forms, anatase: rutile: amorphous = 74:14:8; specific surface area, ~50 m2/g) was available from Degussa Corporation. D-glucose and citric acid were of analytical grade and purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Company.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs was synthesized through hydrothermal carbonization method, using citric acid and glucose as precursors, respectively. In a typical synthesis, as shown in Fig. 1, 1.17 g citric acid or 1 g glucose, which have the same mole of carbon atoms, was dissolved in 600 mL of deionized water with the aid of stirring, and then transferred into a 1000 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and further kept at 170 °C for 12 h. After that, the obtained solution was concentrated to 60 mL in a drying oven at 70 °C, then was filtered with 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove large, biomass-based aggregates. Finally, the CQDs were collected by dialysis against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (3500 MWCO) for 108 h, and concentrated to 40 mL for further experiments. For brevity, the CQDs synthesized from citric acid and glucose were denoted as CQDs-CA and CQDs-G, respectively.

The as-prepared CQDs were further coupled with P25 to prepare CQDs/TiO2 composites by a hydrothermal method. As shown in Fig. 2, in a typical procedure, 0.2 g of P25 was dispersed in 40 mL aqueous solution of CQDs, followed by adjusting the pH to 3–4 with hydrochloric acid, and then mixed with 20 mL of ethanol. The suspension was stirred for 6 h and then transferred into a 100 mL teflon-sealed autoclave and maintained at 150 °C for 6 h. Finally, the resulting composites were collected by centrifugation (15 min at 10,000 rpm), washed with distilled water and ethanol for two times and fully dried at 70 °C for 24 h. Based on the precursors for preparing CQDs, the obtained composites were denoted as CQDs-CA/P25 and CQDs-G/P25, respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany，CuKα radiation, λ = 1.5406 A) was employed to identify the phase composition and crystal structure of the as-prepared samples, using a scanning rate of 8°min−1 and the 2θ degree ranging from 10° to 80°. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured at room temperature on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Japan). The morphology and structural characterization of the obtained CQDs and CQDs/P25 samples was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, America) with accelerating voltage of 300 kV. Raman spectra characterization was carried out by Horiba Jobin Yvon (LabRAM) under He-Ne laser (excitation wavelength: 532.8 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed with a Thermofisher ESC ALAB 250Xip photoelectron spectrometer using monochromatize Al Kα source. All the binding energies were referenced to the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV.

Photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed in a 250 mL beaker containing 100 mL of phenol solution (15 mg/L) and 0.1 g of photocatalyst. The mixture was firstly stirred for 2 h in the dark to ensure the establishment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium before UV light irradiation. During the irradiation (254 nm UV, optical power density of 2.0 mW/cm2), about 7 mL of the suspension was continually taken from the beaker every 1 h, and then centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst particles. Subsequently, the solutions were analyzed with UV–visible spectrophotometer to obtain the relative concentration variation of the solutions.

Fig. 3 displays the typical TEM images of the as-prepared CQDs-CA and CQDs-G, respectively. As observed, the CQDs synthesized from different precursors both exhibit uniform spherical shape, narrow size distribution and almost no clear aggregations, while the CQDs-CA showed a much smaller particle size (2–4 nm) than that of CQDs-G (3–6 nm). The HRTEM images of CQDs-CA and CQDs-G both offer the lattice planes with the interfringe spacing of 0.21 nm, which agrees well with the (100) crystal planes of graphitic carbon [24]. The above observations clearly indicate that the CQDs with high crystallinity were successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal carbonization method.

Raman spectroscopy is widely used for the characterization of the structure of carbon products. As shown in Fig. 4, two typical Raman bands located at about 1370 and 1600 cm−1 can be detected for both CQDs-G and CQDs-CA, which are associated with D-band and G-band of carbon structures, respectively [25]. The D-band is a common feature for sp3 defects in carbon, and the G-band provides information in plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbons [26]. In general, the peak intensity of D-band and G-band can reflect the content of disordered carbon structure and ordered graphite structure, respectively. In other words, the higher intensity ratio of G-band to D-band generally indicate the higher degree of graphitization and better crystalline. Based on the Raman spectra, the IG/ID of CQDs-CA and CQDs-G can be calculated as 1.4995 and 1.7624，respectively. Thus, it can be suggested that CQDs-G has better crystalline structure than CQDs-CA, and a higher electronic transfer ability can be expected for the CQDs-G.

The distinct crystallinity difference between CQDs-CA and CQDs-G is highly connected with the process of hydrothermal carbonization. Fig. 5a and b show the possible hydrothermal formation process of CQDs-CA and CQDs-G, respectively. In the case of CQDs-CA, citric acid molecules were used as precursor, and the possible pyrolysis process of citric acid can be described as Fig. 5a. The citric acid molecules are self-assembled through intermolecular dehydration, and then nanocrystalline GQDs with abundant -OH, C˭O and -COOH groups are formed [27,28]. The formation of CQDs-G follows the similar hydrothermal carbonization process. However, it is widely accepted the CQDs-G formed from glucose generally takes two steps: firstly, glucose transform into the dehydrated intermediate, 5-hydroxymethyl-furfural-1-aldehyde (HMF) [29,30], then HMF molecule is further dehydrated to form the carbonaceous structure through aldol reaction. The intermolecular dehydration of citric acid is more difficult than that of HMF due to the difficulty to get H from -CH2-, which means the carbonization of citric acid is less completely than that of glucose. As a result, the CQDs-G exhibits better crystallinity than CQDs-CA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs/P25 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6a and b show the TEM images of CQDs-CA/P25 and CQDs-G/P25, respectively. It is found that CQDs are uniformly distributed throughout the surface of P25 nanoparticles. The insets in Fig. 6a and b further show the HRTEM images of CQDs-CA and CQDs-G, respectively. The crystal lattice spacing around 0.21 nm agrees well with the (100) spacing of graphitic carbon, while the lattice spacing around 0.25 nm, 0.32 nm, 0.35 nm are assigned to the (101), (110) planes of rutile TiO2 and the (101) plane of anatase TiO2. In addition, it also can be clearly observed that two kinds of CQDs are firmly absorbed on the surface of P25 to form strongly coupled CQDs-CA/P25 and CQDs-G/P25, indicating the well combination between the crystallized CQDs and P25.

The crystal phases of the pure P25, CQDs-CA/P25 and CQDs-G/P25 were characterized by XRD and shown in Fig. 7. All samples exhibit the mixing phases of anatase and rutile TiO2, which are in well accordance with the results of TEM. No typical diffraction peaks of carbon species are detected in CQDs-CA/P25 and CQDs-G/P25 samples, which is mainly due to the low content of CQDs in the composites [12,31]. The XRD results strongly suggested the combination of CQDs and P25 doesn’t change the crystal structures and particle size of TiO2.

In order to further confirm the combination of CQDs and P25, Raman spectra analysis of pure P25 and the composited CQDs-G/P25 were carried out. As shown in Fig. 8, strong peaks at 145 cm−1, 398 cm−1,515 cm−1, 634 cm−1 are detected in the sample P25, which are correspond to the vibrational mode of Eg(1), Eg(2), B1g(1), A1g+B1g(2) and Eg(3) of anatase TiO2, respectively. After coupling with CQDs, the CQDs-G/P25 presents additional significant peaks at 1370 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1. These peaks can be ascribed to the typical D-band and G-band of carbon atoms, further indicating the CQDs are successfully coupled with P25.

XPS measurements were performed to determine the surface elemental characteristics of the CQDs/P25. The binding energy (BE) was calibrated with respect to the C1s value (284.6 eV) of contaminated carbon. Fig. 9 shows the typical XPS survey spectra of CQDs-G/P25. The full survey spectrum (Fig. 9a) indicates the presence of titanium (Ti 2p), carbon (C1s), and oxygen (O 1 s) in the CQDs-G/P25 composites, further suggesting the combination of CQDs and P25. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 9b) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6, 284.9, 286.0 and 288.5 eV. Among them, the peak at around 284.9 eV is assigned to carbon adsorbed on the surface of the CQDs as a contaminant [32]. The binding energy peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.5 eV are ascribed to C-C/C˭C bonds, C-O and C˭O bonds, respectively. The Ti2p spectrum (Fig. 9c) can be fitted to two peaks at 458.6 and 464.3 eV, which are corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, respectively. No peak of Ti-C bond is found in both Ti2p and C1s spectra, indicating that the preparation of CQDs/P25 will not result in the carbon doping into TiO2. In the high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 9d), the peaks at 529.6, 530.1 and 530.7 eV are ascribed to Ti˭O, C˭O and C-O-H, respectively. The XPS results further indicate the successful combination between CQDs and TiO2 in the CQDs-G/P25 composites.

The PL emission spectra of P25, P25/CQDs-CA and P25/CQDs-G were investigated to determine the separation efficiency of charge carriers. Generally, a stronger PL intensity represents a higher recombination probability of the electron-hole pairs under light irradiation [31,33]. As shown in Fig. 10 all the samples show a broad PL emission spectra in 350–550 nm region, which can be assigned to the radiative recombination of self-trapped exciting [34]. It can be observed that the two CQDs/P25 composites both exhibit lower emission intensity than that of pure P25, indicating the introduction of CQDs can efficiently accelerate the separation of electron-hole pairs. In comparison, the CQDs-G is more efficient to promote the separation of charge carriers than that of CQDs-CA. This observation is in well agreement with the results of Raman spectra, where we have demonstrated the CQDs-G exhibits better crystalline structure and corresponding higher electronic transfer ability than that of CQDs-CA.

The photocatalytic activity of P25, CQDs-G/P25 and CQDs-CA/P25 were characterized by the photodegradation of the phenol aqueous solution under UV light irradiation. Before the photocatalytic reaction, the aqueous suspension was stirred for 2 h in a dark condition to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Fig. 11 presents the relative concentration variation of phenol solutions with P25, CQDs-G/P25 and CQDs-CA/P25, respectively. After 2 h dark adsorption treatment, P25 shows almost no adsorption for phenol. However, due to the existence of CQDs, 3.1% and 4.1% of phenol are adsorbed by CQDs-G/P25 and CQDs-CA/P25, respectively. It can be clearly observed that both CQDs-CA/P25 and CQDs-G/P25 exhibit better photocatalytic than that of pure P25, indicating the CQDs can effectively promote the separation of electron-hole pairs and further improve the photocatalytic efficiency. Between the two CQDs-P25 composites, CQDs-G/P25 shows a better photocatalytic activity than that of CQDs-CA/P25, achieving degradation ratio as high as ~99% after 6 h ultraviolet irradiation. Combined with the results of Raman and PL emission spectra, it can be concluded that the CQDs synthesized using glucose as precursor present better crystalline structure and higher electronic transfer ability than that using citric acid, resulting in the enhancement of photocatalytic efficiency after coupling with TiO2.

On the basis of the above results and discussion, a possible mechanism for the accelerated separation of charge carriers and enhanced photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/TiO2 composites is illustrated in Fig. 12. Firstly, the π-π interaction between conjugated structure of CQDs and phenol is beneficial to the enrichment of phenol on the surface of CQDs/P25 composites [14], resulting in the strong adsorption in dark adsorption treatment. In addition, CQDs serve as electron reservoirs to trap photogenerated electrons from the conduction band of P25, which promotes the efficient separation of electrons and holes, and therefore enhances the photocatalytic activity [35,36]. Besides, it should be noted that the electron transfer ability of CQDs is closely related to their crystalline distinction, since the good crystalline means the excellent conjugated graphene network and high electrical conductivity [37,38]. As demonstrated by Raman characterization, CQDs-G has better crystalline structure than CQDs-CA, which eventually leads to the higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and the better photocatalytic activity of CQDs-G/P25 than that of CQDs-CA/P25.

Based on the above results and discussion, the following conclusions can be drawn:
(i)
The two kinds of CQDs using glucose (CQDs-G) and citric acid (CQDs-CA) as carbon source, respectively, both exhibit uniform spherical shape, narrow size distribution and almost no clear aggregations.
(ii)
CQDs-G show higher degree of graphitization and better crystalline, which could be ascribed to the possible mechanism that the intermolecular dehydration of glucose is easier than that of citric acid.
(iii)
The good combination of P25 and CQDs were confirmed by TEM, Raman and XPS analysis. After coupling with CQDs, the composited CQDs/P25 exhibits weaker PL intensity and higher photocatalytic degradation activity of phenol than pure P25 under UV light irradiation, which can be ascribed to the enhanced absorptivity of pollutants and higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs.
(iv)
Moreover, CQDs-G/P25 shows higher photocatalytic activity than that of CQDs-CA/P25, it can be found that the better crystalline of CQDs-G is beneficial for the transfer of electrons from TiO2 to CQDs and leads to higher photocatalytic activity of the composites.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFC0700901, 2016YFC0700607), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. FRF-TP-15–079A1) and the Project (BZZ14J001).
